narration: Define a function called binarySearch that accepts an array and a key.
Initialize an integer variable n for low, set its initial value to zero.
Initialize a variable hi whose initial value is the last index, calculated as length minus one.
Initialize a variable mid to zero.
Write a while loop that continues as long as low is less than or equal to high.
Calculate the midpoint using the formula: mid = (low + high) / 2.
Check if the key value is equal to the element at the mid position. If so, return the index.
If the key value is less than the element at mid, set high to mid minus one.
If the loop completes without finding the element, return -1 to indicate the key is not present.
Explain that binary search requires the array to be sorted before performing the search.
Explain that if the array is not sorted, one must first sort it using an algorithm like selection sort, insertion sort, or bubble sort.
Explain that the best sorting algorithm has a time complexity of O(log n), making the overall complexity O(n log n) when sorting and searching.
